title: Project Bootstrap and Configuration Loading (+15 more)

stories.title: Project Bootstrap and Configuration Loading
stories.description: As a developer, I want COSA to load and validate configuration from appliance.yaml and .env on startup so that the agent has all required parameters before starting any services.
acceptanceCriteria: main.js reads appliance.yaml and .env on startup
All required fields (SSH config, email credentials, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, operator email) are validated at startup
Process exits with a descriptive error if any required field is missing
Config is exposed as a singleton accessible by all modules
Database migrations run automatically after config is loaded

inspectionAssertions.message: Configuration service file must exist for loading and validating appliance.yaml and .env
ConfigService class must be exported
loadConfig method must load configuration from appliance.yaml and .env
validateConfig method must validate loaded configuration
Configuration loading must reference appliance.yaml
Configuration loading must reference .env file
main.js must import ConfigService for startup bootstrap
Configuration loading must be invoked during application startup

stories.title: session.db Schema and Migration
stories.description: As a developer, I want the SQLite session database to be created and migrated automatically so that all agent activity can be persisted from the first run.
acceptanceCriteria: session.db is created at data/session.db if it does not exist
WAL mode is enabled on the database
All five tables are created: sessions, turns, tool_calls, approvals, alerts
FTS5 virtual table turns_fts is created and linked to turns
All indexes defined in the spec are created
Migrations are idempotent (safe to run multiple times)

stories.title: Session Store CRUD Operations
stories.description: As a developer, I want a session-store module that encapsulates all reads and writes to session.db so that other modules never interact with the database directly.
acceptanceCriteria: createSession(sessionId, trigger) inserts a row into sessions
saveTurn(sessionId, role, content, tokensIn, tokensOut) inserts a row into turns
saveToolCall(sessionId, toolCall, result, status) inserts a row into tool_calls
recordBlockedToolCall(sessionId, toolCall, reason) inserts a blocked tool_calls row
createApproval(approvalData) inserts a row into approvals
findApprovalByToken(token) returns the matching approval row
updateApprovalStatus(approvalId, status, resolvedBy, note) updates the approval row
closeSession(sessionId, summary) sets status=complete and summary on the session row
All writes use parameterized queries (no SQL injection risk)

stories.title: SSH Backend with Connection Pool and Reconnect
stories.description: As a developer, I want an SSH backend module that maintains a persistent connection to the Baanbaan Pi and reconnects automatically on failure so that tools can reliably execute remote commands.
acceptanceCriteria: exec(command) runs a shell command on the Baanbaan Pi and returns { stdout, stderr, exitCode }
isConnected() returns the current connection state
Reconnect attempts use exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, max 30s
Command timeout is enforced using the value from appliance.yaml (default 30s)
Connection parameters (host, port, user, key_path) are read from appliance.yaml
command argument is never constructed from user input or LLM output — enforced by module contract
SSH connectivity is tested at startup; failure is logged as a warning but does not crash the process

inspectionAssertions.message: SSH service module must be created for backend SSH operations
SSHService class must be exported from the SSH service module
connect method must establish persistent connection to remote host
executeCommand method must execute remote commands over SSH
reconnect method must implement automatic reconnection on failure
disconnect method must properly close SSH connections
IPC handlers must be registered for SSH operations
JSDoc documentation must be used for public methods per convention

stories.title: Security Gate — Dangerous Command Detection and Output Sanitization
stories.description: As a developer, I want a security gate module that blocks dangerous tool calls and strips credentials from tool output so that destructive or sensitive operations never reach the appliance or the LLM conversation.
acceptanceCriteria: check(toolCall) tests the stringified tool input against all patterns in appliance.yaml security.dangerous_commands
Returns { blocked: true, reason, pattern } when a pattern matches
Returns { blocked: false } when no pattern matches
Pattern matching is case-insensitive
sanitizeOutput(output) replaces Anthropic API keys, generic secrets, password=, and token= patterns with [REDACTED]
Each dangerous command pattern has both a positive test case (should block) and a negative test case (should not block)
100% unit test coverage

inspectionAssertions.message: Security gate module file must exist
isDangerousCommand function must be implemented to detect dangerous tool calls
sanitizeOutput function must be implemented to strip credentials from tool output
isDangerousCommand must be exported as public interface
sanitizeOutput must be exported as public interface
Module must include JSDoc documentation for public functions

stories.title: Tool Registry — Registration, Schema Validation, and Dispatch
stories.description: As a developer, I want a tool registry that registers tools by name, validates inputs against their JSON schema, and dispatches calls to the correct handler so that the orchestrator has a single, consistent interface for all tool invocations.
acceptanceCriteria: register(name, schema, handler) adds a tool to the registry
getSchemas() returns tool definitions in the Anthropic API tool_use format
dispatch(name, input) validates input against the tool's JSON schema before calling the handler
dispatch throws a structured error if the tool name is unknown
dispatch throws a structured error if input fails schema validation
Only tools with enabled: true in appliance.yaml are registered and returned by getSchemas()
90% unit test coverage

stories.title: health_check Tool
stories.description: As COSA, I want to run a comprehensive health check against the Baanbaan appliance so that I can report its status to the operator or trigger an alert.
acceptanceCriteria: Performs SSH connectivity check first; all subsequent steps depend on it
Makes direct LAN HTTP GET to {appliance_api.base_url}/health with 10s timeout
Makes direct LAN HTTP GET to {appliance_api.base_url}/health/ready with 10s timeout
Runs systemctl show baanbaan --property=ActiveState,SubState,ExecMainStartTimestamp,NRestarts via SSH
Parses key=value output from systemctl and computes uptime_seconds from ExecMainStartTimestamp
Returns overall_status: 'healthy' only when SSH connected, both HTTP checks return 200 with ok status, and systemd unit is active/running with 0 restarts
Returns overall_status: 'degraded' for partial failures (non-ok HTTP body, recent restarts)
Returns overall_status: 'unreachable' when SSH fails or HTTP completely fails
Output matches the defined JSON schema
Risk level is 'read' — no approval required

stories.title: db_query Tool
stories.description: As COSA, I want to execute read-only SQL SELECT queries against the Baanbaan SQLite database via SSH so that I can answer operational questions about orders, revenue, and application state.
acceptanceCriteria: Accepts a query string and optional limit (default 50, max 100)
Rejects queries that do not start with SELECT
Rejects queries containing DROP, DELETE, UPDATE, INSERT, CREATE, ALTER, or ATTACH
Appends LIMIT {limit} if not already present in the query
Executes via SSH: sqlite3 -json -readonly {database.path} "{query}"
Returns { rows, row_count, truncated, query_time_ms }
Sets truncated: true when row_count equals the limit
Risk level is 'read' — no approval required
Query timeout enforced per appliance.yaml (default 15s)

stories.title: Approval Engine — Token Generation, FSM, and Expiry
stories.description: As COSA, I want an approval engine that generates secure tokens, tracks approval state, and enforces timeouts so that high-impact actions are never executed without operator consent.
acceptanceCriteria: requiresApproval(toolCall) returns 'auto' for read-risk tools and 'once' for medium/high/critical
requestApproval(sessionId, toolCall, policy) creates an approval record in session.db and sends an approval request email
Token format is APPROVE-{8 uppercase hex chars} generated via crypto.randomBytes(4)
Tokens are single-use and unique
Default approval timeout is 30 minutes; urgent timeout is 5 minutes
processInboundReply(msg) handles APPROVE-TOKEN, DENY, and ambiguous replies
Approved token executes the waiting action and sends confirmation email
Denied approval logs the operator note and sends cancellation email
Background expiry check runs every 5 minutes; expired approvals trigger notification email
All FSM state transitions (pending → approved/denied/expired) are logged to session.db
100% unit test coverage for all FSM transitions

stories.title: Email Gateway — IMAP Polling and SMTP Sending
stories.description: As COSA, I want an email gateway that polls Gmail for inbound messages and sends plain-text SMTP replies so that the operator can interact with COSA via email.
acceptanceCriteria: IMAP polls the COSA inbox every 60 seconds
Only processes messages from the configured operator email address; others are silently ignored and logged
Detects approval replies (APPROVE-TOKEN or DENY pattern) and routes to approvalEngine.processInboundReply
Non-approval messages trigger a new orchestrator session
SMTP send supports to, subject, text, inReplyTo, and references fields
All outbound emails are plain text — no HTML
Email threading headers (In-Reply-To, References) are set correctly on replies
Processed messages are marked as READ in the IMAP inbox
Gmail App Password authentication used (IMAP host: imap.gmail.com:993, SMTP host: smtp.gmail.com:587)
80% unit test coverage with mocked IMAP/SMTP

stories.title: Cron Scheduler — Health Check Task
stories.description: As COSA, I want a cron scheduler that fires a full orchestrator session on a defined schedule so that health checks run autonomously every hour without operator intervention.
acceptanceCriteria: Registers the health-check task with cron expression 0 * * * * from appliance.yaml

If overall_status is 'healthy', no email is sent and the result is logged to session.db
If overall_status is 'degraded' or 'unreachable', an alert email is sent to the operator
Alert deduplication: a new alert for the same category is suppressed if an alert was sent less than 60 minutes ago at the same severity
Alert is recorded in the alerts table with severity, category, title, body, sent_at, and email_to

7997f4fd-3b2a-4f09-92e3-4b1846476506.markdown:
## Context
- Branch: unknown (backend thread)
- Dependencies: none — this is the foundational data layer story (Order 0)
- Relevant existing files: src/main/main.js (entry point), src/main/puffin-state.js (state management pattern reference)
- The project is an Electron app; better-sqlite3 is the assumed SQLite driver (synchronous API, standard for Electron main process)
- The data/ directory at project root is the target location for session.db

## Objective
Implement the SQLite session database bootstrap for the "cosa" project (story: session.db Schema and Migration). This module creates data/session.db on first run, enables WAL mode, and applies all schema migrations idempotently, providing the persistence foundation for all agent session activity including turns, tool calls, approvals, and alerts.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. session.db is created at data/session.db if it does not exist
2. WAL mode is enabled on the database
3. All five tables are created: sessions, turns, tool_calls, approvals, alerts
4. FTS5 virtual table turns_fts is created and linked to turns
5. All indexes defined in the spec are created
6. Migrations are idempotent (safe to run multiple times)

## Codebase Navigation
Before creating any files, use Code Model tools to avoid duplicating existing work:
- Run hdsl_search pattern='database' to check if any db module already exists
- Run hdsl_search pattern='better-sqlite3' to confirm the SQLite package in use
- Run hdsl_search pattern='data/' to find any existing data directory references
- Run hdsl_peek path='src/main/main.js' to understand the app startup sequence and where db init should be wired in
- Run hdsl_peek path='src/main/puffin-state.js' to understand state module patterns before creating the db module
- Run hdsl_search pattern='session' to detect any pre-existing session handling code
- If hdsl_* tools are unavailable, fall back to Grep pattern='better-sqlite3' and Glob pattern='src/main/db/**'

## Implementation Instructions

### Step 1 — Verify package dependency
Check package.json at project root to confirm better-sqlite3 is listed as a dependency. If missing, note it must be added: npm install better-sqlite3. Do NOT proceed without this dependency present. The rest of the instructions assume better-sqlite3 synchronous API.

### Step 2 — Create src/main/db/session-db.js

File path: src/main/db/session-db.js

Purpose: Opens (or creates) data/session.db, enables WAL mode, and runs all schema migrations idempotently. Exports a single openSessionDb function that returns the live database instance.

Exports / Public API:
- openSessionDb(dataDir?: string): Database — accepts optional override path for testing; defaults to path.join(process.cwd(), 'data'); returns the open better-sqlite3 Database instance; throws on failure

Internal logic — implement the following in order:

1. Import path from 'path', fs from 'fs', and Database from 'better-sqlite3'.

2. Define constant DEFAULT_DATA_DIR = path.join(process.cwd(), 'data').

3. Implement openSessionDb(dataDir = DEFAULT_DATA_DIR):
   a. Call fs.mkdirSync(dataDir, { recursive: true }) to ensure data/ exists — this is idempotent.
   b. Construct dbPath = path.join(dataDir, 'session.db').
   c. Instantiate db = new Database(dbPath) — better-sqlite3 creates the file if absent.
   d. Call db.pragma('journal_mode = WAL') to enable WAL mode.
   e. Call db.pragma('foreign_keys = ON') to enforce referential integrity.
   f. Call runMigrations(db).
   g. Return db.

4. Implement runMigrations(db): void (not exported):
   Wrap the entire body in db.transaction(() => { ... })() so all DDL runs atomically. Use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS and CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS for every statement so the function is safe to call repeatedly.

   Create the following tables in this exact order (foreign key order matters):

   TABLE sessions:
     id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
     project_path TEXT NOT NULL,
     branch TEXT,
     status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'active',
     created_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
     updated_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
     metadata TEXT

   TABLE turns:
     id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
     session_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
     role TEXT NOT NULL,
     content TEXT NOT NULL,
     token_count INTEGER,
     created_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
     metadata TEXT

   TABLE tool_calls:
     id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
     turn_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES turns(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
     session_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
     tool_name TEXT NOT NULL,
     input TEXT NOT NULL,
     output TEXT,
     status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
     started_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
     completed_at INTEGER,
     metadata TEXT

   TABLE approvals:
     id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
     tool_call_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tool_calls(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
     session_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
     decision TEXT NOT NULL,
     decided_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
     decided_by TEXT,
     reason TEXT,
     metadata TEXT

   TABLE alerts:
     id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
     session_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
     level TEXT NOT NULL,
     message TEXT NOT NULL,
     created_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
     acknowledged INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
     metadata TEXT

5. Still inside runMigrations, after the five tables, create the FTS5 virtual table:
   CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS turns_fts USING fts5(content, content=turns, content_rowid=rowid)
   Note: turns_fts uses the external content table feature of FTS5, pointing to the turns table. The content column maps to turns.content. After creating the virtual table, create triggers to keep it in sync:

   Trigger turns_ai (after insert on turns): INSERT INTO turns_fts(rowid, content) VALUES (new.rowid, new.content)
   Trigger turns_ad (after delete on turns): INSERT INTO turns_fts(turns_fts, rowid, content) VALUES ('delete', old.rowid, old.content)
   Trigger turns_au (after update on turns): INSERT INTO turns_fts(turns_fts, rowid, content) VALUES ('delete', old.rowid, old.content); INSERT INTO turns_fts(rowid, content) VALUES (new.rowid, new.content)

   Use CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS for all three triggers.

6. Still inside runMigrations, create all indexes using CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS:
   idx_sessions_status ON sessions(status)
   idx_sessions_created_at ON sessions(created_at)
   idx_turns_session_id ON turns(session_id)
   idx_turns_created_at ON turns(created_at)
   idx_tool_calls_turn_id ON tool_calls(turn_id)
   idx_tool_calls_session_id ON tool_calls(session_id)
   idx_tool_calls_status ON tool_calls(status)
   idx_approvals_tool_call_id ON approvals(tool_call_id)
   idx_approvals_session_id ON approvals(session_id)
   idx_alerts_session_id ON alerts(session_id)
   idx_alerts_level ON alerts(level)

  5. Default approval timeout is 30 minutes; urgent timeout is 5 minutes
  6. processInboundReply(msg) handles APPROVE-TOKEN, DENY, and ambiguous replies
  7. Approved token executes the waiting action and sends confirmation email
  8. Denied approval logs the operator note and sends cancellation email
  9. Background expiry check runs every 5 minutes; expired approvals trigger notification email
  10. All FSM state transitions (pending → approved/denied/expired) are logged to session.db
  11. 100% unit test coverage for all FSM transitions
--- END STORY ---

f686bf68-7a29-43a3-b536-ea495f9163b2.markdown:
## Context
- Branch: unknown (verify current branch before starting)
- Dependencies: All Phase 1 implementation stories must be complete before running these scenarios. Required: health-check cron job, email send/receive handlers, approval workflow (APPROVE-TOKEN / DENY / expiry), dangerous-command guard (F-8), session.db persistence, and staging environment config with NODE_ENV=staging 2-minute approval timeout.
- Relevant existing files: src/main/main.js, src/main/ipc-handlers.js, src/main/puffin-state.js, src/main/claude-service.js, src/main/plugin-loader.js. Session database expected at a path resolvable from puffin-state.js. Email transport wired via nodemailer or equivalent in claude-service.js or a dedicated mailer module.

## Objective
This story verifies that all 8 Phase 1 functional scenarios pass end-to-end in a staging environment, serving as the Phase 1 exit gate. It requires creating a staging test harness (test runner script + Baanbaan Pi simulator) that exercises each scenario against the real application stack configured with NODE_ENV=staging, confirming every acceptance criterion before Phase 1 is declared complete.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. F-1: Operator email query returns accurate health status within 2 minutes
2. F-2: Cron health check on healthy appliance produces no outbound email and one session.db row
3. F-3: Cron health check on unreachable appliance sends alert email within 5 minutes
4. F-4: Cron health check on degraded appliance sends degraded alert email with specific warning details
5. F-5: Operator replies with APPROVE-TOKEN — approval status updated and confirmation email sent
6. F-6: Operator replies with DENY — approval status set to denied and cancellation email sent
7. F-7: No operator reply within timeout — approval status set to expired and notification email sent
8. F-8: Dangerous command (DROP TABLE) in tool input is blocked before execution and logged with status=blocked
9. All scenarios verified against a real or simulated Baanbaan Pi exposing the §18.1 health endpoints
10. NODE_ENV=staging uses 2-minute approval timeout

## Codebase Navigation
Before writing any code, use the following queries to understand the existing implementation:
- Run hdsl_search pattern='health' to locate health-check cron logic, health endpoint consumption, and status response shapes
- Run hdsl_search pattern='approval' to find the approval workflow: token generation, APPROVE-TOKEN parsing, DENY handling, and expiry timer
- Run hdsl_search pattern='session' to find session.db schema, the ORM/driver used, and how rows are inserted after a health check
- Run hdsl_search pattern='email' OR pattern='nodemailer' OR pattern='mailer' to find the email send/receive transport and its interface
- Run hdsl_search pattern='dangerous' OR pattern='blocked' OR pattern='DROP TABLE' to locate the command guard that must produce status=blocked log entries
- Run hdsl_peek path='src/main/puffin-state.js' to understand the state shape for approvalStatus, healthStatus, and session records
- Run hdsl_peek path='src/main/ipc-handlers.js' to understand how email inbound events trigger approval-flow transitions
- Run hdsl_peek path='src/main/claude-service.js' to understand how tool inputs are validated and how outbound emails are dispatched
- Run hdsl_deps path='src/main/main.js' to see what the entry point wires together before deciding where to inject the staging timeout override

## Implementation Instructions

### 1. File: tests/staging/baanbaan-simulator.js
Purpose: Lightweight HTTP server that impersonates a Baanbaan Pi by exposing the §18.1 health endpoints. Used by all 8 scenarios so tests do not require physical hardware.

Exports:
- startSimulator({ port, mode }) => Promise<{ server, stop }>
  - port: number (default 7777)
  - mode: 'healthy' | 'unreachable' | 'degraded'
  - Returns server instance and async stop() function

Internal logic:
- Use Node's built-in http.createServer (no external deps).
- Mount GET /health: respond 200 JSON { status: 'ok', uptime: <seconds>, mode: 'healthy' } when mode='healthy'.
- When mode='degraded': respond 200 JSON { status: 'degraded', warnings: ['disk usage 92%', 'temperature 78C'], uptime: <seconds> }.
- When mode='unreachable': do NOT bind the port at all — call server.close() immediately after creation so connection attempts fail with ECONNREFUSED. Log 'simulator: unreachable mode, port not bound'.
- Mount GET /health/details: mirror the same payload as /health for all modes.
- Export a helper setMode(mode) that swaps the response at runtime without restart, stored in a module-level variable currentMode.
- All response payloads must be parseable as JSON with Content-Type: application/json.

Integration: Required by tests/staging/run-scenarios.js. No coupling to src/.

### 2. File: tests/staging/email-interceptor.js
Purpose: Captures outbound emails sent by the application during staging tests without actually delivering them. Also simulates inbound replies (APPROVE-TOKEN, DENY) by invoking the app's inbound-email handler directly.

Exports:
- install() — monkey-patches or stubs the mailer module used in claude-service.js (identified via hdsl_search) to push sent messages into an in-memory array.
- getSentEmails() => Array<{ to, subject, body, timestamp }> — returns all captured emails since last reset.
- clearSentEmails() — resets the array.
- simulateInboundReply({ replyText, token }) — calls the inbound email handler (ipcMain equivalent or direct function export found via hdsl_peek) with a synthetic email body containing replyText and the approval token.
- uninstall() — restores original mailer.

Internal logic:
- After hdsl_search locates the mailer module (e.g., src/main/mailer.js or inline in claude-service.js), wrap its primary send function: const originalSend = mailer.send; mailer.send = async (opts) => { sentEmails.push({ ...opts, timestamp: Date.now() }); };
- simulateInboundReply must construct a minimal email object matching whatever shape the inbound handler expects (inspect via hdsl_peek on ipc-handlers.js).

Integration: Imported by run-scenarios.js before the app boots.

### 3. File: tests/staging/db-inspector.js
Purpose: Reads session.db to assert row counts and field values after each scenario.

Exports:
- getSessionRows() => Promise<Array<object>> — returns all rows from the sessions table.
- getLatestRow() => Promise<object|null>
- clearSessions() => Promise<void> — deletes all rows (used between scenarios).
- getApprovalStatus(token) => Promise<string|null> — returns the approvalStatus field for a given token.

Internal logic:
- Identify the database driver and path by running hdsl_search pattern='session.db' and hdsl_peek on puffin-state.js.
- Use the same driver (likely better-sqlite3 or sqlite3) and path resolution as the app. Do NOT duplicate config — require the same config module the app uses.
- getSessionRows: SELECT * FROM sessions ORDER BY createdAt DESC.
- clearSessions: DELETE FROM sessions.
- getApprovalStatus: SELECT approvalStatus FROM sessions WHERE token = ? LIMIT 1.

Integration: Imported by run-scenarios.js.

### 4. File: tests/staging/run-scenarios.js
Purpose: Main staging test runner. Boots the app in staging mode, runs all 8 scenarios sequentially, reports PASS/FAIL per scenario, exits with code 0 if all pass or 1 if any fail.

Exports: none (executable script, run with node tests/staging/run-scenarios.js)

Internal logic — startup:
- Set process.env.NODE_ENV = 'staging' before any require of app modules.
- Set process.env.APPROVAL_TIMEOUT_MS = '120000' (2 minutes) if not already set by staging config.
- Set process.env.BAANBAAN_HOST = 'http://localhost:7777' (simulator address).
- Import install() from email-interceptor.js and call it.
- Require the app entry point (src/main/main.js) or the cron/health-check module directly — whichever is the minimal boot path. Use hdsl_deps to confirm.
- Wait 500ms for initialization.

Scenario runner helper:
- async function runScenario(name, fn): wraps fn() in try/catch, logs PASS or FAIL with error message, increments pass/fail counters.
- After all scenarios, log summary and process.exit(failCount > 0 ? 1 : 0).

Scenario F-1 — Operator email health query:
- Start simulator in 'healthy' mode on port 7777.
- Call simulateInboundReply with body text 'health status' (or whatever keyword the app monitors — confirm via hdsl_search pattern='email query').
- Wait up to 120 seconds, polling getSentEmails() every 2s.
- Assert: at least one email sent with subject or body containing health status information.
- Assert: elapsed time < 120000ms.

Scenario F-2 — Cron healthy, no email, one db row:
- clearSessions(). clearSentEmails(). Set simulator mode='healthy'.
- Trigger the health-check cron job directly (export a triggerHealthCheck() from the cron module — if not exported, use hdsl_peek to find the function and call it via require).
- Wait 3000ms.
- Assert: getSentEmails().length === 0.
- Assert: (await getSessionRows()).length === 1.

Scenario F-3 — Cron unreachable, alert email within 5 minutes:
- clearSessions(). clearSentEmails(). Start simulator with mode='unreachable' (port not bound).
- Trigger health-check cron.
- Poll getSentEmails() every 2s for up to 300 seconds.
- Assert: one email sent with subject or body indicating unreachable/failure.
- Assert: elapsed < 300000ms.

Scenario F-4 — Cron degraded, degraded alert with warning details:
- clearSessions(). clearSentEmails(). Set simulator mode='degraded'.
- Trigger health-check cron.
- Wait 5000ms.
- Assert: one email sent.
- Assert: email body contains 'disk usage 92%' AND 'temperature 78C' (the exact warning strings returned by the simulator).

Scenario F-5 — APPROVE-TOKEN reply:
- clearSessions(). clearSentEmails().
- Insert a pending approval session row directly via db-inspector or trigger the approval-request flow. Capture the token from getLatestRow().
- Call simulateInboundReply({ replyText: 'APPROVE-' + token, token }).
- Wait 3000ms.
- Assert: (await getApprovalStatus(token)) === 'approved'.
- Assert: getSentEmails() contains one email with subject/body confirming approval.

Scenario F-6 — DENY reply:
- clearSessions(). clearSentEmails().
- Insert pending approval session. Capture token.
- Call simulateInboundReply({ replyText: 'DENY', token }).
- Wait 3000ms.
- Assert: (await getApprovalStatus(token)) === 'denied'.
- Assert: getSentEmails() contains cancellation email.

Scenario F-7 — Expiry timeout:
- clearSessions(). clearSentEmails().
- Set process.env.APPROVAL_TIMEOUT_MS = '3000' (3 seconds for test speed).
- Insert pending approval session. Capture token.
- Wait 6000ms (2x timeout).
- Assert: (await getApprovalStatus(token)) === 'expired'.
- Assert: getSentEmails() contains expiry notification email.
- Restore process.env.APPROVAL_TIMEOUT_MS = '120000'.

Scenario F-8 — Dangerous command blocked:
- clearSessions(). clearSentEmails().
- Obtain a reference to the command-guard function (hdsl_search pattern='blocked' to find it). Call it with a tool input containing 'DROP TABLE users'.
- Assert: the function throws or returns a result with status='blocked'.
- Query the log or session.db for a record where status='blocked' and input contains 'DROP TABLE'.
- Assert: the command was never forwarded to execution (confirm via hdsl_peek on the guard module).

Error handling in run-scenarios.js:
- Each scenario is isolated by clearSessions() and clearSentEmails() at start.
- If the simulator server throws during startup, catch and fail the relevant scenario with message 'simulator failed to start: ' + err.message.
- If a poll loop exceeds its maximum wait, fail with 'timeout waiting for condition'.

### 5. File: config/staging.js (create only if hdsl_search pattern='staging' shows no existing staging config)
Purpose: Exports staging-specific overrides loaded when NODE_ENV=staging.
Exports: { approvalTimeoutMs: 120000, baanbaanHost: process.env.BAANBAAN_HOST || 'http://localhost:7777' }
Integration: Loaded by puffin-state.js or main.js where the approval timeout is read. Verify the config loading path with hdsl_peek before creating; if a config loader already exists, add staging case to it instead.

### 6. Modification: package.json
- Add script: "test:staging": "NODE_ENV=staging node tests/staging/run-scenarios.js"
- No other changes.

## Conventions
- Use camelCase for all variable and function names; JSDoc for all exported functions with @param and @returns tags.
- Follow the IPC handler return pattern from CLAUDE.md: { success: true, data } or { success: false, error: message }.
- Do not use external test frameworks (Jest, Mocha) in the staging runner — keep it plain Node.js with manual assertions using console.assert or throw new Error('assertion failed: ...').
- Poll with setInterval/clearInterval and wrap in a Promise with setTimeout for deadline enforcement; do not use busy-wait.
- All timing constants (poll interval, scenario timeout) should be declared as named consts at the top of run-scenarios.js for easy adjustment.
- Mirror the session.db driver and connection pattern exactly as found in puffin-state.js — do not introduce a second database connection strategy.
- The email interceptor must restore the original send function in uninstall() to avoid cross-contamination if the test runner is imported multiple times.
- Staging test files live under tests/staging/ and must not import from each other circularly.
- After each scenario, log the scenario name, result (PASS/FAIL), and elapsed time in milliseconds to stdout.

prompt.system:
You are generating a Refined Implementation Specification (RIS) document. The RIS is the SOLE instruction document given to a coding agent (Claude Code CLI) to implement a user story. The coding agent has NO other context — it relies entirely on the RIS for what to build, how to build it, and how to verify it.

Your RIS must be a complete, detailed, unambiguous implementation guide. Think of it as a senior developer writing step-by-step instructions for a capable but context-less implementor. Every file, every function, every data flow must be spelled out.
